explicit_text: My mother was from Chiba Prefecture, which is near Tokyo. When we visited Japan in 1999 to trace our family roots, we learned more about her background there. She was quite a spirited person, always involved with her students and enjoying activities like fishing and abalone picking. Her energy and love for life influenced me and my sister a lot growing up.

explicit_text: My father was from Okayama Prefecture, which is known for its pottery. Specifically, they made Bizen ware there, a type of traditional pottery. Although I didn’t know much about him growing up since he passed away when I was very young, learning about his origins connected me to that rich cultural heritage.

Although I didn’t know much about him growing up since he passed away when I was very young, learning about his origins connected me to that rich cultural heritage.

It’s part of the cultural background I carry with me.

explicit_text: I was almost sixteen when Pearl Harbor was attacked on December 7, 1941. We heard the news on the radio one Sunday and were shocked and bewildered that Japan would attack such a big country like the United States. After that, the neighbors’ attitudes changed drastically, with some shunning us and calling us names, though a few, like our Italian neighbors, remained kind and supportive.
implicit_text: I was almost sixteen when the naval base in Hawaii was attacked on December 7, 1941. We heard the news on the radio one Sunday and were shocked and bewildered that Japan would attack such a big country like the United States. After that, the neighbors’ attitudes changed drastically, with some shunning us and calling us names, though a few, like our Italian neighbors, remained kind and supportive.

explicit_text: During World War II, my family and I were forced to leave our home and were sent to an internment camp. My brother, who was like a parent to us, prepared us as best he could, but I was naive and thought it would be a short trip. When we arrived, the harsh reality of living in barracks made of black tar paper hit me hard. We stayed there for an unknown length of time, not knowing what our future held, and it was a difficult and bewildering experience.

explicit_text: Before the war, I participated in the Nisei Week talent shows when I was about 13 and 14 years old. My brother loved music too, so he encouraged me to sing, and I was asked to perform two years in a row. This experience helped me gain confidence, and when I was later in the internment camp, people remembered my singing and asked me to perform at various functions.

explicit_text: Although I didn’t mention Manzanar by name in our conversation, the camp where we were interned was a place where I met many Japanese American youths and made lifelong friends. We formed a club called The Modernaires and shared music and activities to keep our spirits up during those hard times. It was a stark contrast to my earlier life, but it became a significant part of my story.
